content.full_text:
Farn Polrucal PnacrrcEs CouulssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 r Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
December 6,2004
David Davis
1226 W est Valerio Street
Santa Barbara, CA 93101
Re
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-04-242
DearMr. Davis:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the'.Act,).r
QUESTION
Will your position on the board of Community Environmental Council ("CEC'),
an Internal Revenue Service 501(c)(3) environmental education nonprofit, disqualiff you
from decisions regarding a redevelopment project before the Santa Barbara Metropoliian
Transit District ("MTD') in which CEC maybe an applicant?
CONCLUSION
No. You are an unpaid board member of CEC. As such, you have no financial
interest in CEC and, thus, will ha.ve no conflict of interest in decisions regarding CEC.
FACTS
You are a public official, a board member of the Santa Barbara Metropolitan
Transit District ("MTD'). MTD is a public transit district with five bomd merrbers, two
appointed by the county bomd of supervisors, two appointed by the Santa Barbara City
Council, and one public member selected by the four appointed MTD board members.
I Government Code sections 81000 - 9l}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-04-242
Page No. 2
You were appointed by the Santa Barbara County Board of Supervisors as one of their
two appointees.
You are also a board member of the community environmental council ('CEC').
CEC is a communitybased (Internal Revenue 501(c)(3) environmental educational
nonprofit. There are approximately 19 board members from the community at large. The
board position is unpaid (in fact, you provide an annual donation to them). You have no
financial interest or involvement with CEC other than your volunteering as a board
member and yourprivate donations.
The City of Santa Barbara is a charter city with seven council memb€rs. The
council also sits as the redevelopment agency (*RDA') which oversees a downtown
project area. The City of Santa Barbara owns and operates a swface level public parking
lot which covers approximately three-quarters of a cityblock. On one sidsof thelot is a
separate parcel which is owned by the MTD and is the site of the downtown transit
center, a central hub for all bus activity in the city. The transit center is over 25 years old
and is in need ofrenovation. Recently, the MTD staffwas approached by the City of
Santa Barbara staffwith the idea of a joint redevelopment project. Such a project would
entail combining the lands of both entities and re-plarming and expanding the activities
on the site to:
l. Rebuild and expand the public parking on the site by decking the parking
areas.
2. Rebuild a new transit center and realign bus on and offstreet bus circulation,
including possible abandonment of a portion of the street for bus loading and
unloading.
3. Construct affordable housing in the air space above theparking.
4. Construct ancillary commercial space for sale or lease fronting the adjacent
streets.
The RDA has issued tax-exempt bonds which would fund the rebuilding and
expanding of the public parking on site by decking the parking areas; RDA tax increment
would fund the constnrction of affordable housing in the air space above the parking;
MTD would use Federal Transit Assistance ("FTA') andlor Transit Development Act
("TDA') funds forthe kansit center development.
The CEC Board is proposing to request that the city's redevelopment agency
("RDA') consider providing space for their nonprofit administrative offices and a
sustainability education center within the parameters of consbruction of the ancillary
commercial space for sale or lease fronting the adjacent streets. CEC would most likely
purchase their space within the proposed redevelopment project (air-space condominium)
or seek a.long-term lease from the city. CEC is also interestod in working with MTD on
prdects involving alternative fuels in the future.
No specific plan or details of the arrangements betwean the city, MTD, and/or
CEC are available. Everything is conceptual at this time. You anticipate that a joint


FileNo. A-04-242
Page No. 3
meeting of the MTD board and RDA board will happen in early December at which time
it will be determined if there is interest in pursuing this project. CEC will then request
that their involvement as outlined above be considered.
ANALYSIS
Conflict of Interest
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own finansial interests
or the financial interests ofpersons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).)
Specifically, section 87100 prohibits anypublic official from making participating'in
making, or otherwise using his or her offisial position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest-
A public official has a financial interest in a governmental decision within the
meaning of the Act if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will
have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests.
(Section 87103; regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted a standard, eight-
step analysis for deciding whether an individual has a disqualifoing conflict of interest in
a given governmental decision. (Regulation I 8700OX I )-(g).)
Stepl-Publicofficial.
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to 'fublic officials."
(Sections 87100, 87103 regulation 18700(bxl).) "Public official" is defined as..every
member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency . . . .,,
(Section 82048.) A "local govemment agency''means a county, city or district of any
kind, including a school district, or any other local political subdiviiion or any county
board or commission. (section 8204L) As a board me,mber for the MTD, yor, *" u
'lublic official" forpurposes of the Act (see sections 82A4l,8}C/;g),and the conflict-of-
interest rules apply to you.
Step 2 - Making, participating in making or using an ollicial position to
influence governmental decisions.
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only where a public official
"make[s], participate[s] in making or in any way attempt[s] to use his official position to
influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a
financial interest." (Section 87100; regulation ls700OX2).) The commission has
adopted a series of regulations which define'tnaking,,,..participating in making,,, and
"influencing" a governmental decision, and which provide certain exceptions.
(Regulations I 8702- 1 87 02.4.)
I fublic official "makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or


File No. A-04-242
Page No. 4
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.L) A public official
"participates in making a govenrmental decision" when, acting within the authority ofhis
or her position and without significant substantive or interening review, the official
negotiates, advises or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the
govenrmental decision. (Regulation18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his
or her official position to influence the decision i{, for the pu{pose of influencing the
decision, the official contacts, or appears before any member, officer, employee, or
consultant ofhis or her agency. (Regulation 18702.3.) you will bemaking or
participating in making governmental decisions if you participate and vote on decisions
regarding the proposed redevelopmant project.
Step 3 - Identifying the economic interests.
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising
from economic interests as defined in regulations 18703-18703.5. Identifuing which, if
any, of these economic interests are held by a public official is the third step in analyzing
a potential conflict of interest under the Act. (See regulation 18700(b)(3).) There are six
kinds of such economic interests:
A public of,ficial has an economic interest in a business entity in
which he or she has a direct or indirect investmenl of $2,000 or
more (section 87 I 03 (a); regulation I 8703. I (a));
A public official has an economic interest in abusiness entity in
which he or she is a director, officer, partner, tnrsteq employee, or
holds any position of management (section 87 I 03 (d); regulation
18703.1(b));
A public official has an economic interest in real property in
which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more
(section 871 03(b); regulation 187 03.2);
!
A public official has an economic interest in any source of income,
inclludingpromised income, which aggregates to $500 or more
within 12 months prior to the decision (section 87103(c);
regulation 18703.3);
A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifis to
him or her if the gifts aggregate to $340 or more within 12 months
prior to the decision (section 87103(e); regulation 18703.4);
2 An *indirect investunent or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse or
dependent child ofa public official, by an agent on behalfofa public official, or by a business intity or
tntst in which the official, the official's agents, spouse, and dependent children own directly, indireitly, or
beneficially a l0-percent interest or greater." (Section 87103.)


File No. A-04-242
Page No. 5
I I public official has an economic interest in his or her personal
expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those ofhis or
her immediate family. This is known as the "personal financial
effects" rule (sectio n 87 I 03 ; regul ation I 87 03 .5).
Of the six economic interests, only two appear to be potentially implicated by
your facts. Those two categories are "source of income" (section SZf bf(c); regulation
18703.3) and serving as a director or officer of a business entity (section 87103(d);
regulation 18703.1(b)). With respect to source of income, you state that you receive no
compensation for your time on the board.r Additionally, with respect to being a director
or officer of a business entity, a nonprofit organization is not considered a business entity
under the Act. (Section 82005.) You have no economic interest in CEC and, therefore
no financial interest in decisions regarding CEC before the MTD.
Steps 4 - 8.
We have not gone on to describe the latter five steps because we have concluded
that you do not have a financial interest in the decision. For your future reference, we
have enclosed a pamphlet entitled, "Can I Vote?" that explains the entire eight-step
process.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (91 6)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
e-drttG-^ne
Oh. ta.<a.<rc
By: Adrianne Korchmaros
Political Reform Consultant II
AKJg
I:\Adviceltrs\04-242
Enclosure
3 We have no indication in yourrequest for advice that you receive reimbursement for kavel
expenses from CEC. However, even if that were the case, section 82030(b)(2) provides that income does
not include "[s]alary and reimbursement for exp€nses or per diem received from a state, local, or federal
government agency and reimbursement for travel expenses and per diem received from a bona fide
nonprofit entity exempt from taxation under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code.' (Section
82030(bx2).)
